source_metadata.title_raw: Mr. Francis LaSage - A-82-032 - February 22, 1982 - Escalon

content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5660        322-5660           322-3901            332-6441                    572-6444

February 22, 1982


Mr. Francis LaSage
2115 E. Yosemite Avenue
Escalon, CA  95320

Dear Mr. LaSage:

Thank you for your telephone call requesting advice
from our office. As you stated to me, you are planning to
run for a City Council seat. You are currently a volunteer
reserve police officer for the City and your wife is employed
by the City as a clerk typist. Your question is whether the
Political Reform Act would prohibit you from running for
this position.

The Political Reform Act does not prohibit anyone from
running for office, rather, it deals with decision-making by
officeholders. If you are elected to the council seat, you
will be subject to the disclosure and disqualification pro-
visions of the Act.

With respect to the disqualification provisions, a
public official is forbidden from making, or participating
in the making of, or in any way attempting to use his official
position to influence a governmental decision in which he official
knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest
(Government Code Section 87100).1/ A copy enclosed). An official
has a financial interest within the meaning of Section 87100
if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
a material financial effect on: Any source of income
(Section 87103(c)).2/  Under the Act, "income" includes an
individual's community property interest in his spouse's
income (Section 82030(a)).


1/ All statutory references made are to the Government
Code unless otherwise noted.

2/ See the enclosed copy of Section 87103 for other
types of specified interests.

Mr. Francis LaSage
February 22, 1982
Page Two


     At first glance, it would appear that if you became a
City Councilmember you would have to abstain on any votes
concerning your wife's salary.  Section 82030(b)(2), however,
alters the outcome.  This section provides an exception
which states, "(b) Income ... does not include ... (2)
Salary ... received from a ... local ... government agency...."
Therefore, your wife's wages are exempt from the definition
of income and you have no financial interest in decisions
affecting it.

     With respect to your job as a volunteer police reserve
officer, you receive no income and, thus, have no financial
interest which would require you to abstain from participating
in decisions affecting that position or the police department.

     If you have any additional questions, please feel free
to call me at 916/322-5901.

                                        Very truly yours,

                                        [signature]
                                        Janis Shank McLean
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

JSM:km
Enclosure

sections.question_synthetic: Would the Political Reform Act prohibit the requester from running for City Council given that he is a volunteer reserve police officer and his wife is employed by the City as a clerk typist?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Political Reform Act does not prohibit anyone from running for office. If elected, the requester would be subject to disclosure and disqualification provisions, but because his wife's government salary is exempt from the definition of income under Government Code Section 82030(b)(2), and he receives no income as a volunteer officer, he would have no financial interest requiring abstention from most decisions.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Would the Political Reform Act prohibit the requester from running for City Council given that he is a volunteer reserve police officer and his wife is employed by the City as a clerk typist?

CONCLUSION: The Political Reform Act does not prohibit anyone from running for office. If elected, the requester would be subject to disclosure and disqualification provisions, but because his wife's government salary is exempt from the definition of income under Government Code Section 82030(b)(2), and he receives no income as a volunteer officer, he would have no financial interest requiring abstention from most decisions.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to a candidate for City Council asking whether the Political Reform Act would prohibit his candidacy given his status as a volunteer police officer and his wife's employment as a city clerk typist.
